skill.name: use-dom
skill.description: Use Expo DOM components to run React web code in native WebViews and on web without rewrites.
skill.summary: Guide Expo teams through DOM components, WebView setup, props, routing, assets, and platform behavior.
skill.author: expo

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but the import, command, crypto, reconnaissance, and path traversal matches are false positives from Markdown documentation and TypeScript examples. A medium-risk semantic issue remains because the skill demonstrates exposing native async functions to DOM WebView code without warning about input validation or trust boundaries.

medium_findings.title: Native Bridge Validation Guidance Missing
medium_findings.description: The skill shows native async functions passed into a DOM component and called from WebView code. This is a legitimate Expo pattern, but it can expose native storage, alerts, or other app capabilities to untrusted web content if callers do not validate inputs and restrict actions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code examples clearly pass native callbacks into a DOM component and execute them from WebView-side code. The risk depends on how developers use the pattern, so this is a warning rather than evidence of malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Dynamic Import Static Matches Are Type-Only Examples
low_findings.description: The detected import() expressions are TypeScript type annotations for expo/dom DOMProps inside documentation examples. They are not runtime dynamic imports and do not load attacker-controlled modules.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Each match appears in a TypeScript prop type such as dom: import("expo/dom").DOMProps. The surrounding code is fenced Markdown example content, not executable skill code.

low_findings.title: Backtick Command Matches Are Markdown Formatting
low_findings.description: The detected Ruby or shell backtick execution matches are Markdown inline code spans or fenced code blocks. No evidence found of shell execution by the skill itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is documentation and uses backticks to format identifiers, libraries, file names, and code examples. There is no script file or command runner in the reviewed skill content.

low_findings.title: Relative Asset Require Is Not Path Traversal
low_findings.description: The path traversal match is a documented React Native asset import using require("../assets/logo.png"). The path is static and local to the example project.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The require call is a hardcoded asset reference in a Markdown example. It does not accept user input or access arbitrary filesystem paths.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto And Reconnaissance Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography hit appears in the word DOM inside the skill description. The reconnaissance hits describe platform behavior and component data, not system probing.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines discuss Expo DOM components, chart axes, route parameters, and Android WebView behavior. No cryptographic API or host reconnaissance command is present.

dangerous_patterns.title: WebView To Native Callback Bridge
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation demonstrates WebView-side code invoking native async callbacks. Developers should validate arguments, limit exposed actions, and avoid connecting this pattern to untrusted remote content.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The bridge pattern is explicit in the examples, but the skill does not show remote content or malicious use. The concern is misuse risk in downstream apps.

content.value_statement: React Native teams often need web-only libraries inside Expo apps. This skill shows how to use DOM components for charts, editors, routing, assets, and incremental migration.
actual_capabilities: Explains when Expo DOM components fit better than native components.
Shows the required use dom directive and single default export rule.
Demonstrates typing and configuring the special dom prop.
Covers native async callbacks passed into DOM components.
Shows use of web libraries, CSS imports, routing props, and assets.
Summarizes platform behavior across iOS, Android, and web.
limitations: It is documentation only and does not install Expo packages.
It does not cover every WebView security setting.
It assumes the project already uses Expo and React components.
It does not replace native UI guidance for performance-critical screens.

use_cases.title: Migrate Web Widgets
use_cases.description: Move existing React web widgets into an Expo app while preserving their DOM APIs and CSS.

use_cases.title: Use Web-Only Libraries
use_cases.description: Add charting, syntax highlighting, editors, or embeds that depend on browser APIs.

use_cases.title: Plan Hybrid Screens
use_cases.description: Decide which parts of a screen should stay native and which parts can run inside a DOM component.

prompt_templates.title: Create A Basic DOM Component
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a new component.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an Expo DOM component for this UI. Include the use dom directive, typed props, and the dom prop.

prompt_templates.title: Wrap A Web Library
prompt_templates.scenario: Use a browser-dependent React package in Expo.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me wrap this React web library as an Expo DOM component. Explain required imports, props, and sizing.

prompt_templates.title: Connect Native Actions
prompt_templates.scenario: Bridge a DOM component to native app behavior.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a safe native callback interface for this DOM component. Include validation rules and limited actions.

prompt_templates.title: Audit A Hybrid Screen
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Expo screen plan. Decide what should be native, what should be DOM, and what risks need mitigation.

output_examples.input: A developer wants to add a web-only chart library to an Expo app.
output_examples.output: A component plan with the DOM file boundary, typed data props, dom sizing, and native screen usage.

output_examples.input: A team wants to pass route parameters into a DOM component.
output_examples.output: Guidance to read route state in the native parent and pass serializable values into the DOM component.

output_examples.input: A lead asks whether a full screen should become a DOM component.
output_examples.output: A recommendation to keep navigation and simple UI native, with only specialized web content inside DOM components.

best_practices: Keep each DOM component focused on one web-specific feature.
Pass only serializable props and validate native callback inputs.
Test rendering and performance on iOS, Android, and web.
anti_patterns: Do not convert simple native UI into WebViews without a web-only need.
Do not expose broad native actions to DOM code.
Do not place DOM component logic inside layout route files.

faq.question: What does this skill help build?
faq.answer: It helps build Expo DOM components that run web React code inside native WebViews and directly on web.

faq.question: Can DOM components use browser-only libraries?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill shows examples for charting, syntax highlighting, rich editors, CSS, canvas, and WebGL use cases.

faq.question: Should entire screens use DOM components?
faq.answer: Usually no. Keep native navigation and simple UI native, then use DOM components for specialized web content.

faq.question: How should routing state reach a DOM component?
faq.answer: Read routing state in the native parent and pass serializable values as props.

faq.question: Can DOM components call native code?
faq.answer: Yes. Pass limited async functions as props, then validate every input before native work happens.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with a medium warning about WebView-to-native callback validation. No malicious behavior was found.
